content.problemStatement:
In the middle of a certain ocean, there are nnn pillars rising high out of the waters. These pillars are connected by an ancient network of n−1n-1n−1 bridges, each at a height of 10910^9109 above sea level. It is possible to travel between any pair of pillars using these bridges, that is, the pillars are connected\textit{connected}connected through the ancient bridges.

At the current time, there are a total of mmm bridges. This is because, in the modern era, the inhabitants of these pillars have built m−(n−1)m-(n-1)m−(n−1) additional ad hoc bridges to increase the convenience of travel. However, due to the technological decline, each of these ad hoc bridges only has a height of between 000 and 5⋅1085 \cdot 10^85⋅108 above sea level.

The inhabitants want their pillars to be biconnected\textit{biconnected}biconnected: that is, the pillars should be connected even if any one bridge breaks down. However, they know that the seas may rise in the future, rendering bridges with height strictly below this new water level useless. Determine the maximum water level at which the pillars are still biconnected.

Additionally, the inhabitants plan to build qqq more bridges in the future in an order 1,2…q1, 2 \dots q1,2…q. Similarly to the ad hoc bridges, each of these bridges have a height of between 000 and 5⋅1085 \cdot 10^85⋅108 above sea level. After each additional bridge, report the new maximum water level at which all the pillars are still biconnected.


Input Format

The first line contains two space-separated integers nnn and mmm, the number of pillars and the total number of bridges at the current time respectively.
The next n−1n-1n−1 lines of input each contain two space-separated integers. The iiith of these lines contains uiu_iui​ and viv_ivi​, representing an ancient bridge of height 10910^9109 between uiu_iui​ and viv_ivi​.
The next m−(n−1)m-(n-1)m−(n−1) lines of input each contain three space-separated integers. The i−(n−1)i-(n-1)i−(n−1)th of these lines contains uiu_iui​, viv_ivi​ and wiw_iwi​, representing an ad hoc bridge between uiu_iui​ and viv_ivi​ of height wiw_iwi​.
The next line contains one integer, qqq, the number of bridges to be built in the future.
The next qqq lines each contain three space-separated integers xix_ixi​,yiy_iyi​ and ziz_izi​, representing a bridge to be constructed in the future between xix_ixi​ and yiy_iyi​ of height ziz_izi​.


Output Format
Output q+1q+1q+1 integers, one per line. The first line should be the maximum allowable water level at the current time. For all 1≤i≤q1 \leq i \leq q1≤i≤q, the (i+1)(i+1)(i+1)th line should contain the maximum allowable water level after the iiith future bridge has been constructed.


If, at any point, the pillars are not biconnected even with a water level of 000, output −1-1−1 for the respective line instead.


Subtasks
The input is divided into multiple subtasks. You will get the points allocated for a subtask if and only if you solve every testcase in the subtask correctly, within the time limit.


Please remember that when you make a submission to a problem, we will automatically consider your submission for every single subtask, and also the full problem. After the contest, your scores will be 'stitched'. This means that if you solve a subtask in any of your submissions, you will get the points for the subtask in your final score. There is no need to combine your solutions for different subtasks into the same submission.


For all subtasks:



2≤n≤1052 \leq n \leq 10^52≤n≤105
1≤m≤2⋅1051 \leq m \leq 2 \cdot 10^51≤m≤2⋅105
0≤q≤1050 \leq q \leq 10^50≤q≤105
1≤ui,vi≤n1 \leq u_i,v_i \leq n1≤ui​,vi​≤n for all iii
1≤xi,yi≤n1 \leq x_i,y_i \leq n1≤xi​,yi​≤n for all iii
0≤wi,zi≤5⋅1080 \leq w_i,z_i \leq 5 \cdot 10^80≤wi​,zi​≤5⋅108 for all iii

These are the subtasks for the problem:


**For all subtasks except Subtask 7, q=0q=0q=0 (i.e., there will be no future bridges considered).**



Subtask 1 [4 points]: n≤250n \leq 250n≤250, m=nm=nm=n, ui=iu_i = iui​=i, vi=imod  n+1v_i = i \mod n + 1vi​=imodn+1 for all iii
Subtask 2 [6 points]: n≤250n \leq 250n≤250, m=nm=nm=n
Subtask 3 [20 points]: n≤250n \leq 250n≤250, m≤500m \leq 500m≤500
Subtask 4 [17 points]: n≤1000n \leq 1000n≤1000, m≤3000m \leq 3000m≤3000
Subtask 5 [17 points]: n≤4000n \leq 4000n≤4000, m≤8000m \leq 8000m≤8000
Subtask 6 [17 points]: No additional constraints.
Subtask 7 [19 points]: No additional constraints.
constraints: All ancient bridges have height exactly 10^9 (they are never removed by any water level).
content.inputFormat: The first line contains two integers n and m – the number of pillars and the total number of bridges that exist right now. The next n‑1 lines each contain two integers u v describing an ancient bridge of height 10^9 between pillars u and v. The following m‑(n‑1) lines each contain three integers u v w describing an ad‑hoc bridge of height w (0 ≤ w ≤ 5·10^8). The next line contains an integer q – the number of bridges that will be built in the future. The next q lines each contain three integers x y z describing a future bridge of height z that will be added after the previous ones (the i‑th line corresponds to the i‑th future bridge). Pillars are numbered from 1 to n.
content.outputFormat: Output q+1 lines. The first line is the maximum water level (an integer) such that, after discarding every bridge whose height is strictly smaller than this level, the whole set of pillars is still biconnected (i.e. the remaining graph stays connected after removal of any single bridge). For each i from 1 to q, the (i+1)-th line must contain the same value after the i‑th future bridge has been added. If at some moment the pillars are not biconnected even when the water level is 0, output -1 for that line.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
At a water level of 444, only the bridge between 222 and 444 with weight 222 is unusable. It is possible to verify that the pillars will still be biconnected with only the remaining bridges.
Note that the pillars are defined as biconnected if they are still connected even after the deletion of any single bridge.

solution.approach:
The problem asks for the largest threshold L such that the subgraph consisting of all bridges with height ≥ L is 2‑edge‑connected. Adding bridges never hurts – it can only keep or improve the situation – therefore the answer for a later time is never smaller than the answer for an earlier time.

We solve all queries offline with *parallel binary search* (also called batch binary search). For every time moment t (t = 0 … q) we keep a search interval [low[t], high[t]] for the answer. Initially low[t] = 0 and high[t] = 10^9 (the height of the ancient bridges). In each iteration we compute mid[t] = (low[t] + high[t] + 1) / 2 for all still‑undecided times and group the times by the same mid value.

For a fixed value X we have to know, for every time t, whether the graph formed by **all bridges whose height ≥ X and whose appearance time ≤ t** is 2‑edge‑connected. This can be answered in O((n + number_of_edges)·α) using a *DSU that also maintains the number of bridges* (see CP‑Algorithms "DSU with support for bridges"). The DSU supports two operations:
  * addEdge(u, v) – inserts a new edge and updates the internal bridge count;
  * snapshot / rollback – restores the DSU to a previous state.

To answer many different times for the same X we use a segment tree over the time axis. An edge e that appears at time te and has height w is relevant for all queries with threshold X ≤ w and for all times t ≥ te. When we are processing a particular X, we insert e into the segment‑tree nodes that cover the interval [te, q] (the whole suffix where the edge is present). While traversing the segment tree we keep a DSU state, push the edges stored in the current node, recurse to children, answer the queries that belong to the leaf (the concrete time), and finally roll back the DSU to the state before entering the node. This gives us the answer (is the graph 2‑edge‑connected?) for every time in O((n + total_edges)·α·log q).

The parallel binary search repeats the above procedure O(log H) times (H ≤ 10^9, i.e. ≤ 30). Overall complexity is O((n + m + q)·α·log q·log H) which easily fits the limits (≈ 2·10^5·30·log 10^5). The memory consumption is O(n + m + q·log q).

If at some moment the graph is never 2‑edge‑connected even for L = 0, the binary search will end with low = -1; we output -1 for that moment.

**Key data structures**
* DSU with bridge support – stores parent, size, 2‑edge‑connected component representative, and the current number of bridges.
* Rollback stack – records every change made to the DSU so that we can revert in O(1).
* Segment tree of vectors – each node keeps the list of edges that are active for the whole interval represented by the node.

The algorithm works for the full constraints and also for the easier subtasks (where q = 0 the segment tree degenerates to a single leaf, i.e. a simple DSU run).
solution.timeComplexity: O((n + m + q) · α(n) · log q · log H) where H ≤ 10^9 (≈30) and α is the inverse Ackermann function.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(n + m + q·log q) for DSU, rollback stack and segment‑tree containers.
